content:
# Disagreements — Genesis 42 (GEN_042)

## 1. Vayyitnakker — "Made Himself Unrecognizable" vs. "Acted as a Stranger" (v. 7)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "made himself unrecognizable to them" | Hithpael of n-k-r = reflexive concealment |
| LOGOS | "made himself unrecognizable to them" | Same — preserves the n-k-r root thread |
| PNEUMA | "acted as a stranger toward them" | Captures the social performance dimension |
| **SOPHIA** | **"made himself unrecognizable" — 2-1** | The n-k-r root thread (37:32 hakker-na → 38:25 hakker-na → 42:7 vayyitnakker) is one of the most significant lexical links in the Joseph cycle. "Unrecognizable" preserves the recognition/non-recognition meaning central to the root. |

## 2. Ervat Ha'arets — "Nakedness of the Land" vs. "Where the Land Is Exposed" (v. 9)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the nakedness of the land" | Literal — preserves ervah's force |
| LOGOS | "the nakedness of the land" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "where the land is exposed" | Dynamic — explains the military metaphor |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the nakedness of the land" — 2-1** | The word ervah carries connotations of shame and vulnerability beyond mere strategic exposure. "Where the land is exposed" explains away the metaphor's strangeness. The MT's striking phrase should be preserved. |

## 3. Keinim — "Honest Men" vs. "Upright Men" (v. 11)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "upright men" | ken = upright, straight, honest |
| LOGOS | "honest men" | Natural English in context of refuting spy charge |
| PNEUMA | "honest men" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"honest men" — 2-1** | In context of refuting espionage, the brothers are asserting their sincerity and truthfulness. "Honest" is the more natural English equivalent. |

## 4. Et-Ha'Elohim Ani Yare — Word Order (v. 18)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the God I fear" | Preserves Hebrew word order |
| LOGOS | "I fear God" | Natural English |
| PNEUMA | "I am a man who fears God" | Dynamic expansion |
| **SOPHIA** | **"I fear God" — LOGOS upheld** | EMET's word order sounds awkward in English. PNEUMA adds words not in the text. LOGOS is natural and accurate. |

## 5. Vayyetse Libbam — "Heart Failed" vs. "Hearts Went Out" vs. "Hearts Sank" (v. 28)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "their heart failed" | Vivid English idiom for the Hebrew image |
| LOGOS | "their hearts went out" | More literal rendering of vayyetse |
| PNEUMA | "their hearts sank" | Different English idiom |
| **SOPHIA** | **"their heart failed them" — EMET upheld** | The Hebrew literally says "their heart went out" — the image of the heart leaving the body in fear. "Their heart failed them" captures the visceral panic of the idiom better than either "went out" or "sank." |

## 6. Hayyeled — "Child" vs. "Boy" (v. 22)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the child" | yeled = child, young one |
| LOGOS | "the boy" | Joseph at 17 is better described as "boy" |
| PNEUMA | "the boy" | Same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the boy" — 2-1** | Joseph was seventeen when sold. In English, "boy" is more appropriate for a seventeen-year-old than "child." |

## 7. Beit Mishmarkhem — "House of Confinement" vs. "Prison-house" vs. "Prison" (v. 19)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "house of confinement" | Literal — beit mishmar = house of custody |
| LOGOS | "prison-house" | Matches project convention |
| PNEUMA | "the prison" | Simple dynamic rendering |
| **SOPHIA** | **"place of custody" — distinguished from beit haSohar** | The Hebrew beit mishmar (house of custody/guarding) is distinct from beit haSohar (which the project renders "prison-house"). Using "prison-house" for both conflates the terms. "Place of custody" preserves the distinction. |

## 8. Alai Hayu Kullanah — "Come" vs. "Fallen" vs. "Against Me" (v. 36)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "upon me have all these things fallen" | Adds "fallen" for dramatic effect |
| LOGOS | "Upon me have all these things come" | Closer to Hebrew hayu ("were/became") |
| PNEUMA | "everything is against me!" | Dynamic paraphrase |
| **SOPHIA** | **"upon me have all these things come" — LOGOS upheld** | The Hebrew uses hayah ("to be/become"), not nafal ("to fall"). LOGOS's "come" is accurate. PNEUMA's "against me" paraphrases. |

---

*Genesis 42: 8 disagreements logged. SOPHIA ruled with EMET/LOGOS on 2 items (vv. 7, 9), with LOGOS/PNEUMA on 2 items (vv. 11, 22), with LOGOS alone on 2 items (vv. 18, 36), with EMET alone on 1 item (v. 28), and independently on 1 item (v. 19 — "place of custody" distinguishing beit mishmar from beit haSohar). All three translators agreed on the major structural and theological decisions: the prostration formula fulfilling the dream (v. 6), the guilt confession (v. 21), the blood-reckoning (v. 22), and the Sheol reference (v. 38). This was a chapter of high agreement — the disagreements center on register and English idiom rather than theology or interpretation.*
